name: Xolotl Creature Form
description: Xolotl, the Aztec god of lightning, fire, death, and monsters, often manifested in monstrous canine forms. These forms were grotesque and unsettling, reflecting his role as a psychopomp guiding the dead to Mictlan, the underworld. As a creature, Xolotl could take on various terrifying appearances, including a dog-headed man, a skeleton, or a creature with reversed feet. These forms symbolized his liminal nature, existing between the world of the living and the dead. His monstrous forms also highlighted his association with transformation and the dangers of the night. Some depictions show him with empty eye sockets, further emphasizing his connection to the underworld and the unknown. He was a protector of twins and deformed individuals, reflecting his own misshapen appearance and his role as an outcast. He was also associated with the planet Venus as the Evening Star, which follows the sun into the underworld each night, mirroring his journey guiding the dead. The creature form of Xolotl served as a stark reminder of the cyclical nature of life and death and the inherent darkness within the cosmos.
subtitle: Monstrous guide of the dead.
appearance: Xolotl's creature form is typically depicted as a large, monstrous canine, often skeletal or deformed. He may have reversed feet, empty eye sockets, or a generally grotesque appearance. Sometimes he is shown as a dog-headed man or a creature with sharp claws and teeth. His fur, if present, is usually dark or black, symbolizing his association with the night and the underworld. He might also be depicted with flames or lightning emanating from his body, representing his power over these elements.
habitat: Xolotl's creature form is most often found in the underworld (Mictlan) or in liminal spaces between the world of the living and the dead, such as caves, graveyards, and dark forests. He is also associated with places of transformation and transition, such as crossroads and volcanic landscapes.
behavior: Xolotl, in his creature form, is often depicted as a guide and protector, leading the dead through the dangers of the underworld. He is also a trickster and a shapeshifter, capable of manipulating his form to deceive or intimidate others. While primarily benevolent in his role as a psychopomp, his monstrous appearance and association with death can make him appear frightening and unpredictable. He is fiercely loyal to those he protects but can be dangerous to those who disrespect the dead or attempt to interfere with the natural cycle of life and death.

keyMyths.title: The Creation of Mankind
keyMyths.description: Xolotl helped Quetzalcoatl retrieve the bones of previous generations from Mictlan to create mankind. He was instrumental in the escape and rebirth of humanity, showing his connection to both death and creation.

keyMyths.title: Xolotl's Sacrifice
keyMyths.description: During the creation of the Fifth Sun, Xolotl refused to die and transformed into various forms to escape sacrifice, eventually becoming the axolotl. This shows his association with transformation and his struggle against death.

sources.author: H.B. Nicholson
